title: Why digital regulation is now an economic growth question in Asia
gist: Oxford Economics survey and cross-country analysis argue that in Asia, the coherence and design of digital regulation — not just its intent — now materially drives startup formation and VC funding, with 'digital dysregulation' from piling up individually sensible rules creating a real economic drag on innovation ecosystems.

themes.theme: Digital regulation has become a defining growth variable

insights.claim: The number of digital policy changes across Asia rose from 35 in 2018 to 642 in 2025, showing that regulation has shifted from a peripheral issue to a defining feature of the environment in which startups operate.
insights.speaker: Yasuko Koido
insights.so_what: An 18x rise in policy changes in seven years is itself a headline risk factor for Asian tech investors, independent of any single rule's content.
insights.locator: Individual rules shape the wider ecosystem

insights.claim: The concept of 'digital dysregulation' describes how individually sensible regulatory interventions can pull in different directions and gradually reduce the coherence of the overall policy environment, making it harder for firms to invest, innovate, and scale.
insights.speaker: Yasuko Koido
insights.so_what: Reframes regulatory risk assessment away from judging individual rules toward judging cumulative regulatory coherence — a more useful lens for investors screening country-level startup ecosystems.
insights.locator: Individual rules shape the wider ecosystem

insights.claim: The economic impact of digital regulation depends not only on its specific purpose but often more importantly on how it is designed, implemented, and experienced by the firms it affects.
insights.speaker: Yasuko Koido
insights.so_what: Central thesis for evaluating regulatory regimes: implementation quality can matter more than stated intent for real-world investment outcomes.
insights.locator: Individual rules shape the wider ecosystem

themes.theme: Survey evidence: compliance burden on startups

insights.claim: In a survey of 1,050 startups across India, Malaysia, and South Korea, almost nine in 10 said digital regulations made day-to-day operations more difficult.
insights.speaker: Yasuko Koido
insights.so_what: Near-universal reported operational friction across three different regulatory regimes suggests the burden is systemic, not country-specific.
insights.locator: Individual rules shape the wider ecosystem

insights.claim: 77% of pre-revenue startups reported spending more on compliance expertise (such as cybersecurity, data governance, and legal compliance) as regulatory requirements expanded.
insights.speaker: Yasuko Koido
insights.so_what: Pre-revenue startups are the most cash-constrained, so this compliance spending directly cuts into runway and product-development capacity at the most fragile stage.

insights.claim: More than half of surveyed startups reported delays in product development or a longer time to market as a result of digital regulation.
insights.speaker: Yasuko Koido
insights.so_what: Regulatory friction is shown to directly slow product velocity, a competitiveness risk versus less-regulated markets or larger incumbents that can absorb the delay.

insights.claim: Only 4% of venture capital investors surveyed said digital regulation was irrelevant to their decisions, and two-thirds said regulation made it harder to assess likely investment returns.
insights.speaker: Yasuko Koido
insights.so_what: Regulatory uncertainty is shown to directly impair VC underwriting, which can raise the cost of capital or reduce funding availability for startups in affected markets.
insights.locator: Individual rules shape the wider ecosystem

themes.theme: Trust benefits of regulation are uneven

insights.claim: Only around a third of one-year-old startups surveyed said digital rules had strengthened customer trust in their products or services, showing the trust-building benefit regulators often cite is far from universal in practice.
insights.speaker: Yasuko Koido
insights.so_what: Pushes back on the standard justification that regulation primarily builds consumer trust — the actual payoff is inconsistent, weakening the case for regulation-as-trust-building in its current form.
insights.locator: Individual rules shape the wider ecosystem

themes.theme: Cross-country evidence linking restrictiveness to weaker ecosystems

insights.claim: Across a study of digital regulations in 95 countries, more restrictive regulatory environments are associated with weaker new-firm formation and lower venture capital investment growth.
insights.speaker: Yasuko Koido
insights.so_what: Provides cross-country empirical backing (not just survey sentiment) that regulatory restrictiveness has measurable negative effects on startup ecosystem health.
insights.locator: Evidence helps policymakers see the trade-offs

insights.claim: Shifting a country's digital regulatory environment from the more restrictive end of the range to the less restrictive end is associated with a 3 percentage point increase in annual growth in new-firm creation and a 4 percentage point increase in annual growth in VC funding.
insights.speaker: Yasuko Koido
insights.so_what: Quantifies the economic upside of regulatory reform in concrete, comparable percentage-point terms that can be used to model country-level opportunity.
insights.locator: Evidence helps policymakers see the trade-offs

insights.claim: These percentage-point gains translate into thousands of additional businesses and tens of millions of dollars in additional investment across some of Asia's most strategically important innovation sectors.
insights.speaker: Yasuko Koido
insights.so_what: Frames regulatory reform as a large, quantifiable economic growth lever for Asian policymakers, not just a compliance-cost issue.
insights.locator: The economic gains from getting regulation right

insights.claim: The future of technology policy in Asia will be shaped not only by the strength of regulatory ambition but also by the quality of the evidence underpinning it.
insights.speaker: Yasuko Koido
insights.so_what: Argues investors and policymakers should weight evidence-based, coordinated regulatory design as a distinct signal from mere regulatory intensity when assessing a market's startup potential.
